EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Old Amherst Road in Belchertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of the area. Some of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds. Also precipitable water values were near two inches, allowing for heavy rain with all of these storms, which resulted in a few instances of poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was downed by thunderstorm winds onto Rogers Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the midwest, off the mid-Atlantic coast and northeastward across Nantucket and the outer arm of Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.  A strong low level jet associated with the low pressure brought strong winds to the surface.  Winds reached high wind criteria at Logan International Airport in Boston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observation System at Logan International Airport in East Boston, MA recorded surface sustained wind speeds of 41 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary to the west of Southern New England ignited showers and thunderstorms across the area.  Heavy rain further saturated the already wet ground and thunderstorm winds in the 30-40 mph range were enough to push a few trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State police reported winds gusting up to 40 mph downed three trees and some wires, blocking Foundry Village Road.  Heavy rain over the previous several days had saturated the ground allowing for lighter winds downing trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea tracked across southeastern Massachusetts bringing heavy rain (3-5 inches) to much of southern New England.  This resulted in significant urban flooding, particularly across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, as well as river and small stream flooding.  It also contributed to record high rainfall across the area for the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power lines on Arthur Avenue in Dartmouth were downed by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved off the mid Atlantic coast Tuesday night, January 15th, and passed just south of Cape Cod Wednesday afternoon the 16th. Snow associated with this low overspread the Berkshires during the early morning hours Wednesday. Some warmer air worked into the area by early in the afternoon resulting in some sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle. The bulk of the precipitation occurred in the morning in the form of snow and impacted the commute resulting in some accidents. Total snowfall reports across Berkshire County ranged from 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southern New England, igniting showers and thunderstorms in the warm, unstable airmass ahead of the front. With plenty of instability and steep mid level lapse rates, some of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure was situated along the eastern seaboard on July 19th. The flow around this high pressure ridge brought a very warm and humid air mass into western Massachusetts, along with plenty of sunshine.||The result was high temperatures exceeding 90 degrees. When combined with the humidity, heat index values exceeded 100 degrees at times during the afternoon and evening hours. ||Over the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward through Southern New England, resulting in moist southerly flow across much of the area.  Showers and thunderstorms developed across western Massachusetts and southern New Hampshire, producing heavy rainfall towards the end of one of the wettest June's in southern New England history.  This resulted in flash flooding in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple cars became stuck in flood waters on Route 116 and several basements were flooded on North Pleasant Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England igniting showers and thunderstorms across parts of the region.  Most of these storms produced heavy rain and lightning, a couple resulted in localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight lifeguard stands were blown over on Horseneck Beach in Westport.  Also, a large branch was downed onto Balsam Street in Fairhaven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved across the southern New England coastline, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across much of the area.  While the precipitation started as snow for most locations, snow quickly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain across portions of Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeastern Massachusetts.  Areas of northern Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire remained all snow and received around a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to eight inches of snow fell across eastern Hampden County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong arctic cold fronts moved through the region between Wednesday January 9th, 2013 and the early morning hours on Thursday, January 10th, 2013.  With the passage of the final boundary, a weak line of rain and snow showers developed. Strong, gusty winds occurred both accompanying and in the wake of the showers.  A wind gust to 58 mph was recorded at the Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) at Harriman-and-West Airport in North Adams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 58 mph was recorded in North Adams at 12:38 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England igniting showers and thunderstorms across parts of the region.  Most of these storms produced heavy rain and lightning, a couple resulted in localized wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England igniting showers and thunderstorms across parts of the region.  Most of these storms produced heavy rain and lightning, a couple resulted in localized wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A user on Facebook reported nickel to quarter size hail during a thunderstorm in Pittsfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure was situated along the eastern seaboard on July 19th. The flow around this high pressure ridge brought a very warm and humid air mass into western Massachusetts, along with plenty of sunshine.||The result was high temperatures exceeding 90 degrees. When combined with the humidity, heat index values exceeded 100 degrees at times during the afternoon and evening hours. ||Over the weekend, an approaching cold front brought somewhat cooler and less humid air into the region, which allowed heat index values to be lower.

In Fall River, large branches were downed onto wires and a tree was downed onto a house on Highland Avenue.  Also, a 40 foot section of roof was blown off the Westall School on Maple Street. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and humid air mass was in place across the region on Sunday, September 1st. A surface frontal boundary was situated across southern New York into southern New England during the morning hours. During the day, the frontal boundary slowly lifted northward. With enough instability in place due to daytime heating, some showers and thunderstorms developed along this frontal boundary. The showers and thunderstorms tracked over the same locations during the afternoon hours across Berkshire County. As a result of the persistent heavy rain, flash flooding occurred, causing roads to be flooded in the Williamstown area.||By the evening hours, the showers and thunderstorms were located north of the region and beginning to weaken, and the threat for flash flooding ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations along Route 7 in Williamstown experienced flash flooding due to heavy rainfall.  In addition, several other local roads in Williamstown were impacted by flood waters as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Route 136 was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place on Thursday, July 18th. With temperatures exceeding 90 degrees, the atmosphere was very unstable. A weak upper level disturbance passed to the north of the area across southern Canada. Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorm activity diminished in the evening with the loss of daytime heating as the weak upper level disturbance moved away from the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually moist atmosphere, even for early August standards, was in place across the region on the morning of Friday, August 9th.  A cold front, aided by strong upper level dynamics, was approaching the region from the Great Lakes.  ||Ahead of this front, a large area of heavy rainfall and embedded thunderstorms moved across the region.  Areas of heavy rainfall repeatedly moved across the same portions of the Berkshires.  This heavy rainfall of more than three inches in just a few hour period caused streams and creeks to overflow their banks, resulting in flash flooding.  Roads were closed as a result of the flooding and water rushed into some basements.  By the afternoon hours, the heavy rain had ended and the water begun to recede.  ||Much less humid air moved into the region by that night as the cold front swept through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in Williamstown.  Water rushed over roadways and flooded basements.

The third student was further away when the lightning struck and was not directly hit, suffering less serious effects. The students were 16, 17, and 20 years of age. All of the students reovered from their injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a strong cold front, a southerly flow allowed for a warm and humid air mass to move into the region. Temperatures were quite warm for early October, with temperatures reaching well into the 60s. As a cold front moved across the region during the afternoon hours on October 7th, a line of showers and thunderstorms moved from west to east. Strong winds accompanied these showers and thunderstorms, with widespread reports of trees and power lines downed as a result of the thunderstorm winds. There were about 1,700 power outages in western Massachusetts as a result of the thunderstorms. With the passage of the cold front late in the afternoon, the winds switched to a westerly direction as cooler air moved into the region, and the severe threat ended as storms moved eastward and away from the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a car in Stockbridge as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass settled in over the region on January 22nd and remained in place for a few days as cold fronts moved across the region ushering in reinforcing surges of cold air. In addition to the cold air, winds remained rather brisk for much of the time resulting in dangerously cold wind chill values. In addition to the cold air, winds remained rather brisk for much of the time resulting in dangerously cold wind chill values. A weak clipper type low pressure system produced light freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across much of Southern New England.  This resulted in hazardous driving conditions and caught some drivers unaware, resulting in numerous accidents across the area.  A 65 car pile up occurred on Interstate 290 westbound, near exit 14 in Worcester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 car pile up occurred on Interstate 290 westbound near exit 14 in Worcester during the early morning hours.  Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle was occurring at the time, resulting in slick roads.  Thirty-five to forty people were injured, only two seriously.  The crash scene was 1500 feet long and included two tractor trailers and two large commercial trucks.  The interstate was closed for four and a half hours.  Nearby, another accident occurred on Route 9 in Northboro just east of the Route 20 interchange.  Twenty-five cars were involved in that crash but no serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England igniting showers and thunderstorms.  A cold pool aloft allowed for steep mid level lapse rates as well as temperatures cold enough for large hail.  Storms were isolated but produced damaging winds in northern Connecticut and up to half dollar size hail in southwestern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail fell, covering parts of the driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through southern New England, providing a focus for a few showers and thunderstorms.  Instability was limited somewhat by cloud cover and weak winds at the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere resulted in slow moving storms.  The atmosphere was very moist with precipitable water levels around 2 inches, this led to very heavy rainfall with storms that developed.  One particular storm became severe over Greenfield and produced a microburst, downing numerous trees through the northwestern part of the city.  It also produced flash flooding in Greenfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred in the northwestern part of Greenfield and the southeastern portions of Leyden and Colrain, downing numerous trees and wires.  Barton, Country Club, Bernardston, Plain, South Green River, Nelson, and Smead Hill Roads were all affected with a few trees that were downed onto houses.  A utility pole was downed on Barton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Ohio Valley towards western New York on Saturday, December 14th. At the same time, another area of low pressure began to develop over the mid-Atlantic coast late in the day and rapidly strengthened overnight as it moved up the eastern seaboard. This storm was located off the coast of New England during the day on Sunday, December 15th. ||As a result, snow slowly spread from southwest to northeast during the day on December 14th. With a cold and dry airmass in place, the snow was initially light in intensity and little accumulation occurred during the day. ||However, as the secondary low began to rapidly develop, a steadier and heavier snowfall moved across the region during the evening hours and into the overnight. Snow fell at rates in excess of one inch per hour over much of the region.  During the overnight hours, some sleet mixed in with the snow, especially across southern parts of Berkshire County. ||The bulk of the accumulating snow was finished by the late overnight hours, but light snow showers and flurries continued into the mid-morning hours. By the end of the storm, snowfall amounts ranged from nearly around 6 inches in Pittsfield to about 11 inches in Adams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of upper level disturbances moved over southern New England while at the surface a slow moving warm front drifted north across the area.  This ignited showers and thunderstorms across much of the area.  Southwest flow at the surface provided a deep layer of moisture with high dewpoints and high precipitable water values, which resulted in heavy rainfall with the showers and thunderstorms.  Anywhere from one to four inches of rain fell with these storms, sometimes within a very short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 2 and Interstate 495 were flooded with six inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low pressure region (976mb) moved from Ontario into southern Quebec.  Southern New England was sandwiched in between this and high pressure off the coast to the southeast, creating a very tight pressure gradient over the region.  This resulted in strong gusty winds which caused some damage, especially to areas where the trees were still fully leaved.  A few locations sustained higher wind gusts and thus more damage.  These were mainly along the east coast of Massachusetts.

In addition, CAPE up to 2000 J/kg and 30-40 kts of shear provided enough instability and shear for thunderstorms to become severe.  Because of warm air aloft, these thunderstorms mostly produced strong to damaging winds and not large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on Tracey Lane, igniting a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure rises behind a cold front coupled with cold air advection resulted in strong to damaging winds across much of southern New England.  Most of the damage was to trees, some of which were still at least partially leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Fitchburg Municipal Airport (KFIT) recorded a wind gust of 51 mph.  Damage reports indicate that winds were likely higher in other parts of northern Worcester County.  Multiple branches and wires were downed on Lake Street in Leominster.  Trees were downed in Lancaster and Fitchburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved into southern New England, providing lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop in a hot and humid atmosphere.  In addition, CAPE up to 2000 J/kg and 30-40 kts of shear provided enough instability and shear for thunderstorms to become severe.  Because of warm air aloft, these thunderstorms mostly produced strong to damaging winds and not large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree near a house on Morrow Street.  The lightning traveled along the invisible fence line to the house, damaging the invisible fence circuitry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure rises behind a cold front coupled with cold air advection resulted in strong to damaging winds across much of southern New England.  Most of the damage was to trees, some of which were still at least partially leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Plymouth Municipal Airport (KPYM) recorded a wind gust of 43 mph.  In addition, a large tree was downed, blocking Grove Street in Norwell.  A tree was downed onto wires on Richard Road in Hingham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of upper level disturbances moved over southern New England while at the surface a slow moving warm front drifted north across the area.  This ignited showers and thunderstorms across much of the area.  Southwest flow at the surface provided a deep layer of moisture with high dewpoints and high precipitable water values, which resulted in heavy rainfall with the showers and thunderstorms.  Anywhere from one to four inches of rain fell with these storms, sometimes within a very short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Main Street in Cambridge was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge over southern New England resulted in a long period of very hot and humid weather across the region.  July 3rd through July 7th all had high temperatures above 90 degrees, constituting an official heat wave for the city of Boston.  Low temperatures provided little relief, dropping just below 80 degrees for much of the time.  Despite the extended period of high heat and humidity, only one person succumbed to heat illness.  That was a 45 year old postal worker in the city of Medford who died on July 6th.  The official cause of death for the postal worker was heat illness.  On July 6th the high temperature in Boston was 93.  The heat index climbed to 90 degrees by 9am and remained above 90 degrees through 9pm.  From 1pm-5pm, the heat index was 96 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45 year old mail carrier died as a result of heat illness on July 6th.  Temperatures in the area had climbed above 90 for the 3rd day in a row on the 5th, when the mail carrier was found and transported to the hospital.  The high humidity levels resulted in heat indices above 90 degrees for the daylight hours and heat indices above 96 degrees for 8 to 9 hours beginning at 11am.  The event start time indicates when heat indices climbed to 100 degrees and the event end time is the time at which he was found collapsed.  He was pronounced dead the following morning at Massachusetts General Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east coast trough ignited showers and thunderstorms in a hot and humid atmosphere.  Steepening mid level lapse rates and modest CAPE promoted the potential for severe storms while warm mid and upper level temps limited the extent.  At the end of the day, only one storm became severe, producing some minor tree damage and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several eight inch diameter tree limbs on West Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across Southern New England provided enough lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop in the warm, unstable environment ahead of the front.  A few of these storms became severe resulting in both large hail and damaging winds.  Up to one and a half inch diameter hail was reported as well as some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell on Route 133 near the Andover/Lowell town line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved across the southern New England coastline, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across much of the area.  While the precipitation started as snow for most locations, snow quickly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain across portions of Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeastern Massachusetts.  Areas of northern Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire remained all snow and received around a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to ten inches of snow fell across western Norfolk County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator recorded a 58 mph wind gust on their home weather station in Barnstable.  A spotter in East Falmouth recorded a 61 mph wind gust on their home weather station.  The Automated Weather Observation System at Provincetown Municipal Airport (KPVC) recorded sustained wind speeds of 44 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  A stationary band of even heavier snowfall persisted from southwest NH through central MA and on to the southwest across central and western CT. In those areas, reports averaged closer to 2.5 to 3 feet! Along the southeast MA coast, average amounts ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Only on Martha���s Vineyard and Nantucket were snowfall totals less than 1 foot (6 to 12 inches).  Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. The band of heaviest snowfall, with 3 to 5 inches per hour for several hours, extended from southwest NH to central and western CT.  The precipitation started as mainly snow, although a brief period of rain at the onset was common on the Islands.  Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA. It lingered during the whole afternoon over Cape Cod and Nantucket, aided by some ocean-effect bands of snowfall.||The Blizzard of 2013 also produced a prolonged period of very strong winds Friday night along the MA and RI coasts.  Gusts exceeded hurricane force (74 mph) at a few locations.  Gale force gusts (to 50 mph) continued on the MA coast through Saturday afternoon.  The strong winds, combined with a wet snow, led to extensive power outages from downed trees and wires in southeast coastal MA and in southern RI.  Elsewhere, farther inland, the snow became drier and did not cling to trees like it did along the south and southeast coast of New England.  Some wind gusts included: 76 mph at Logan Int���l. Airport (Boston, MA), 75 mph at Bedford, MA, 77 mph at Hyannis, MA and 68 mph in Jamestown, RI.  Damaging gusts to 60 mph were recorded as far west as Worcester County, MA. Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common elsewhere in southern New England.| |In addition, moderate to major coastal flooding occurred, most notably during the time of the high tide Saturday morning along the Massachusetts east coast.  At the storm's height near the early morning low tide, the storm surge reached 3 to 4 feet along much of the MA east coast from Boston south.  At the time of the mid-morning high tide, the winds had shifted from northeast to north and the surge had dropped to 1.5 to 2.5 feet for most MA east coast locations.  However, this was an astronomically high tide given the nearness to the time of the new moon, and waves to 30 feet had built just 15 miles off the coast.  Consequently, many coastal roadways were impassable from Salisbury and Gloucester to Marshfield and Scituate on the south shore and on parts of Cape Cod.  Water several feet deep was seen flowing into some vulnerable homes in Scituate.  Although there was some structural damage, it did not come close to what was experienced during the Blizzard of 1978.  Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were observed on the Automated Surface Observing System at Taunton Municipal Airport (KTAN) for three and a half hours. End time of this event was estimated due to the loss of wind sensors at almost all ASOS stations in eastern MA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  A stationary band of even heavier snowfall persisted from southwest NH through central MA and on to the southwest across central and western CT. In those areas, reports averaged closer to 2.5 to 3 feet! Along the southeast MA coast, average amounts ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Only on Martha���s Vineyard and Nantucket were snowfall totals less than 1 foot (6 to 12 inches).  Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. The band of heaviest snowfall, with 3 to 5 inches per hour for several hours, extended from southwest NH to central and western CT.  The precipitation started as mainly snow, although a brief period of rain at the onset was common on the Islands.  Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA. It lingered during the whole afternoon over Cape Cod and Nantucket, aided by some ocean-effect bands of snowfall.||The Blizzard of 2013 also produced a prolonged period of very strong winds Friday night along the MA and RI coasts.  Gusts exceeded hurricane force (74 mph) at a few locations.  Gale force gusts (to 50 mph) continued on the MA coast through Saturday afternoon.  The strong winds, combined with a wet snow, led to extensive power outages from downed trees and wires in southeast coastal MA and in southern RI.  Elsewhere, farther inland, the snow became drier and did not cling to trees like it did along the south and southeast coast of New England.  Some wind gusts included: 76 mph at Logan Int���l. Airport (Boston, MA), 75 mph at Bedford, MA, 77 mph at Hyannis, MA and 68 mph in Jamestown, RI.  Damaging gusts to 60 mph were recorded as far west as Worcester County, MA. Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common elsewhere in southern New England.| |In addition, moderate to major coastal flooding occurred, most notably during the time of the high tide Saturday morning along the Massachusetts east coast.  At the storm's height near the early morning low tide, the storm surge reached 3 to 4 feet along much of the MA east coast from Boston south.  At the time of the mid-morning high tide, the winds had shifted from northeast to north and the surge had dropped to 1.5 to 2.5 feet for most MA east coast locations.  However, this was an astronomically high tide given the nearness to the time of the new moon, and waves to 30 feet had built just 15 miles off the coast.  Consequently, many coastal roadways were impassable from Salisbury and Gloucester to Marshfield and Scituate on the south shore and on parts of Cape Cod.  Water several feet deep was seen flowing into some vulnerable homes in Scituate.  Although there was some structural damage, it did not come close to what was experienced during the Blizzard of 1978.  Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Martha's Vineyard Airport in Vineyard Haven, MA (KMVY) recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to seven inches of snow fell across western Hampden County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the coast of southern New England coupled with a shortwave/mid level trough in the Ohio Valley resulted in a typical Norlun trough set up across the area.  These troughs can promote a very narrow band of heavy snow where low level convergence sets up.  This can result in areas in the band having much more snow than areas outside of the band.  This set up spread snow across much of the area with the highest amounts occurring along the south coast of Massachusetts and into Newport County, Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County.

Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven to eight inches of snow fell across western Hampshire County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved across the southern New England coastline, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across much of the area.  While the precipitation started as snow for most locations, snow quickly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain across portions of Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeastern Massachusetts.  Areas of northern Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire remained all snow and received around a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to ten inches of snow fell across eastern Essex County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ten to twelve inch diameter tree was downed on Elaine Way in Acushnet.  Wires were downed on Acushnet Avenue in New Bedford.  Amateur radio operators in New Bedford and Fall River recorded wind gusts to 47 mph on their home weather stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the coast of southern New England coupled with a shortwave/mid level trough in the Ohio Valley resulted in a typical Norlun trough set up across the area.  These troughs can promote a very narrow band of heavy snow where low level convergence sets up.  This can result in areas in the band having much more snow than areas outside of the band.  This set up spread snow across much of the area with the highest amounts occurring along the south coast of Massachusetts and into Newport County, Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to five inches of snow fell across northern Bristol County.

Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to sixteen inches of snow fell across eastern Plymouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the coast of southern New England coupled with a shortwave/mid level trough in the Ohio Valley resulted in a typical Norlun trough set up across the area.  These troughs can promote a very narrow band of heavy snow where low level convergence sets up.  This can result in areas in the band having much more snow than areas outside of the band.  This set up spread snow across much of the area with the highest amounts occurring along the south coast of Massachusetts and into Newport County, Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to ten inches of snow fell across Cape Cod.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level low and cold pool aloft moved over Southern New England, steepening mid-level lapse rates and resulting in enough instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop.  A weak, short-lived tornado spun up in one of these thunderstorms near the Bay State Ford dealership in Stoughton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down at a car dealership on Route 138 in Stoughton where it was captured by a security camera and recorded on several employees cell phone cameras.  Three RV trailers, weighing about 5000 pounds each, were pushed back about 6 feet against a chain link fence.  One trailer was lifted about 15 feet into a light pole but landed on its wheels with minimal damage.  An aluminum door about 20 feet high and 15 feet wide was blown out of the service garage.  Eyewitnesses reported ceiling tiles being lifted inside the dealership as the tornado passed overhead.  The tornado entered a wooded area just to the northeast of the dealership.  Roughly one quarter of a mile away on Erica Drive and Dean Road, two large pine trees were downed before the tornado dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front pushing south through New England produced isolated severe thunderstorms in western and central Massachusetts during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 mph was reported in West Cummington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A beaver dam on a stream had a catastrophic failure, sending a substantial amount of water down the stream into the Quaboug River.  This flooded a portion of the river, causing extensive damage to Route 67 in Warren (which had been flooded by a previous beaver dam failure) and resulted in the evacuation of one house which sustained minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A beaver dam on a pond had a catastrophic failure.  This sent a substantial amount of water down a stream and into the Quaboug River, flooding Route 67 and damaging it extensively.  One family was evacuated and the house sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture in place across southern New England ahead of a cold front resulted in precipitable water values increasing to 2-2.5 inches (2 to 3 times normal).  The cold front provided enough lift for widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms across all of southern New England.  The high moisture content of the atmosphere resulted in torrential downpours across much of the region, which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads throughout town were closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the medical office building on the campus of Baystate Mary Lane Hospital in Ware, blowing out the building's electrical panel.  In addition, three people who were on the telephone at the time of the strike reported feeling numbness and a tingling in their arms.  All three walked over to the hospital to be checked out and were fine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea tracked across southeastern Massachusetts bringing heavy rain (3-5 inches) to much of southern New England.  This resulted in significant urban flooding, particularly across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, as well as river and small stream flooding.  It also contributed to record high rainfall across the area for the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea moved across southeastern Massachusetts, resulting in three to six inches of rain falling across Bristol County.  This produced significant street flooding, particularly in Fall River and New Bedford.||In Fall River, the area of town known as Globe Four Corners was flooded.  Cars were stranded in two feet of water at the intersection of Pleasant and Quarry Streets.  A portion of Route 79 was flooded.  The ramp onto Interstate 195 from Central Street was flooded with two feet of water.  The ramp from Route 24 southbound onto Interstate 195 was flooded with two to three feet of water, one car was stuck in floodwaters.  Davol Street was closed due to flooding.  Plymouth Avenue near Davol Street, Jefferson Street, and Warren Street were all flooded.||In New Bedford, Liberty Street was flooded and impassable from Durfee Street to Parker Street.  Several basements were flooded on Cove Road in New Bedford.  There was one foot of standing water at the intersection of West Rodney French Boulevard and Cove Road.  Cove Road was closed due to tidal and freshwater flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region remained in a warm and humid air mass as an upper level ridge remained just south and east across the western Atlantic Ocean. At the same time, a weak upper level disturbance passed by to the north across southern Canada.  This upper level disturbance allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours of Monday, June 24th.  With a moist and unstable atmosphere in place, some of these storms became severe, with large hail and damaging wind gusts.  Thunderstorms dissipated by later in the evening, as the disturbance moved east of the region and the atmosphere stabilized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down and on a wire in Richmond due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and cooling air in the upper levels resulted in steepening mid-level lapse rates, producing showers and thunderstorms.  A warm, unstable environment allowed these storms to become severe, with several storms producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two feet of water flooded Route 1 northbound in Danvers.  In Peabody, a car was stuck in flood waters on Roosevelt Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Westfield, several cars were stuck in flood waters near Westfield Industrial Park Road.  Several water rescues occurred.  East Mountain Road, North Elm Street, and Meadow Street were flooded with cars stuck in the flood waters.  Canal Drive was closed after flooding caused a section of the road to collapse.  In Holyoke, a section of Northampton Road was washed out.  Lyman, Walnut, Cabot, and Commercial Streets were all flooded.  A car was submerged at the intersection of Cabot and Commercial Streets, occupants had to be rescued.  Route 5 (or Riverdale Street) in West Springfield was flooded.  In Chicopee, Broadway at Saint James Avenue was closed because of flooding.  Meadow Street and Meeting House Road also were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through southern New England, providing a focus for a few showers and thunderstorms.  Instability was limited somewhat by cloud cover and weak winds at the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere resulted in slow moving storms.  The atmosphere was very moist with precipitable water levels around 2 inches, this led to very heavy rainfall with storms that developed.  One particular storm became severe over Greenfield and produced a microburst, downing numerous trees through the northwestern part of the city.  It also produced flash flooding in Greenfield.

A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A basement was flooded on Chris Drive.  Five to six inches of water flooded the police station parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough developed over southern New England, providing a focus for low level convection.  Plenty of instability, as well as a strong upper jet aloft, allowed several of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  A very moist, tropical-like atmosphere resulted in very heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding, mainly across southwestern New Hampshire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Mill Village Road and Greenfield Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across southern New England igniting showers and thunderstorms.  A cold pool aloft allowed for steep mid level lapse rates as well as temperatures cold enough for large hail.  Storms were isolated but produced damaging winds in northern Connecticut and up to half dollar size hail in southwestern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail fell on Main Street, accumulating up to two inches in spots.  A car on Fairview Avenue was damaged with numerous hail dents.  There were also several reports of nickel size hail in the area of Riverdale Road.  About 500 cars at the Balise family of car dealerships were damaged.  Damage ranged from minimal to extensive.  Further damage was found two weeks later, when heavy rain fell through small holes in the roof of Chestnut Middle School, resulting in water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, moist airmass remained in place across Southern New England with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  Showers and thunderstorms that developed with an upper level disturbance ahead of a cold front resulted in very heavy rainfall with reports of near 2 inches in 30 minutes in some locations.  This resulted in flooding particularly in southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Attleboro, the intersection of Maple and South Main Streets was flooded.  A street partially collapsed near the new Walmart on Broadway in Raynham.  In New Bedford, Maplewood Avenue flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves moved along a stationary boundary to the west of Southern New England, igniting showers and thunderstorms.  An atmosphere characterized by low CAPE and decent deep layer shear resulted in showers and thunderstorms producing heavy rain and rotating thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced a microburst in Agawam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amesbury Road in Haverhill was flooded.  Jackson and Swan Streets in Methuen were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves moved along a stationary boundary to the west of Southern New England, igniting showers and thunderstorms.  An atmosphere characterized by low CAPE and decent deep layer shear resulted in showers and thunderstorms producing heavy rain and rotating thunderstorms.  One of these storms produced a microburst in Agawam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team from the National Weather Service in Taunton, MA confirmed that a microburst occurred in the town of Agawam.  There were two areas of straight line wind damage.  One was in the area of Harvey Johnson Blvd where several trees were downed, a few onto houses.  The more significant area of damage was the area around Meadow Street where 40 to 50 trees were uprooted and 6 to 12 vehicles were crushed by the fallen trees.  Wires were downed in both areas.  There were no reports of significant structural damage or injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Weatherflow reporting site at Pleasure Bay recorded a wind gust of 69 mph.  The Weatherflow reporting site at Deer Island recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Logan International Airport (KBOS) recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.  In Boston, a tree was downed onto East Street and power lines were downed on Red Pine Road.  A large tree was downed onto cars and laying across Blue Hill Avenue.  A tree was downed at the intersection of VFW Parkway and Centre Street in Jamaica Plain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators in Hingham, Scituate, and Plymouth recorded wind gusts of 65, 60, and 62 mph on their home weather stations, respectively.  The Automated Surface Observing System at Plymouth Municipal Airport (KPYM) recorded wind gusts to 52 mph.  The Weatherflow reporting site at Hull recorded a wind gust to 60 mph.  A Weatherflow reporting site in Duxbury recorded a wind gust to 64 mph.  A tree was downed onto wires on Congress Street.  A tree was downed at the intersection of Derby Street and Route 3 in Hingham.  In Scituate, a large tree was downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Orange Municipal Airport (KORE) recorded a wind gust of 49 mph.  In Montague, a tree was downed onto wires on Dry Hill Road.  A tree was downed onto Route 202 in New Salem.  A tree and wires were downed on Lower Road in Deerfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators in Harwich, Eastham, Barnstable, East Falmouth, Chatham, and Marstons Mills reported wind gusts to 64, 62, 62, 56, 55, and 53 mph, respectively, on their home weather stations.  The Automated Weather Observing System at Cape Cod Coast Guard Air Station in Falmouth (KFMH) recorded wind gusts to 58 mph.  Sustained winds up to 41 mph were reported by the general public in East Falmouth and Eastham.  A Weatherflow reporting site in Eastham recorded a wind gust to 62 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Taunton Municipal Airport (KTAN) reported wind gusts up to 51 mph.  An amateur radio operator also recorded sustained winds up to 49 mph on their home weather station in Taunton.  Numerous trees were downed in Raynham.  Also, a 50 foot portion of the cafeteria roof at the Merrill School on Pleasant Street was blown off.  Numerous trees were downed throughout Attleboro.  One of these trees was downed onto a car on Reservoir Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the midwest, off the mid-Atlantic coast and northeastward across Nantucket and the outer arm of Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.  A strong low level jet associated with the low pressure brought strong winds to the surface.  Winds reached high wind criteria at Logan International Airport in Boston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten inches of snow fell across western Middlesex County.

A strong low level jet associated with the low pressure brought strong winds to the surface.  Winds reached high wind criteria at Logan International Airport in Boston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across western Norfolk County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the Ohio Valley approached the region around midnight on Wednesday, February 27th. Snow, moderate to heavy at times, broke out across the Berkshires during the early morning hours. Light snowfall also developed across the valley areas of western Massachusetts.||Another area of low pressure developed over the mid-Atlantic region and moved northeast inland across the region for the later on the morning of Wednesday, February 27th. The track of this low allowed warmer air, both at the surface and aloft, to move into the region. This allowed the precipitation to change to rain and become spotty across the lower elevations, ending the snow accumulation for these areas.  However, upslope flow allowed steady precipitation to continue across the high terrain of the Berkshires through the day. Although there was some mixing with sleet and freezing rain, the precipitation remained mainly snow for a long enough period of time to allow for moderate accumulations of snow to occur. The steady precipitation tapered off to snow showers during the early morning hours of Thursday, February 28th.||By Thursday, February 28th, storm total snowfall greatly varied according to elevation. While valley areas only had an inch or two, higher elevations across the Berkshires saw up to a foot of snow.

In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No wind speed observations were reported from southern Plymouth County, however reports from surrounding areas and damage reports indicate that winds likely reached high wind criteria.  A car port was blown over in Rochester.

In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.

In addition, the Weatherflow reporting site at Children's Island and an amateur radio operator in Rockport recorded wind gusts to 58 and 50 mph respectively.  A tree was downed in Gloucester, blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Marblehead, a parking lot at Devereux Beach was under a foot of fast moving water and overwash.  Atlantic Avenue was closed near the Swampscott line due to coastal flooding and large debris in the road.  Marblehead Neck Causeway (Ocean Avenue) was flooded, closing the road.  Rocks and other ocean debris blocked the roadway.  In Salisbury, significant beach erosion occurred near North End Boulevard, the south end of Atlantic Avenue, and Beach Center.  Water flowed around houses in these areas.  The dune structure on Salisbury Beach experienced major damage.  Beach Road was closed due to flooding.  The 200 block of North End Boulevard was closed due to coastal flooding.  The 700 block of North End Boulevard to Route 1A was closed due to a breach in the seawall.  Several feet of sand were in the road.  The Surf Side Beach Bar and Grill at 25 Broadway was condemned because of structural damage resulting from battering of waves for multiple tide cycles.  Eleventh Street was flooded.  In Essex, Route 133 was closed because the causeway was flooded.  In Lynn, Nahant Parkway and Lynn Shore Drive were closed due to flooding.  In Rockport, a paved parking area at Old Harbor was undermined.  Splashover over the seawalls at Front and Back Beaches was flowing around buildings and depositing debris in the road.  Penzance Road was completely torn up with sheets of pavement washed away.  A driveway in the Back Beach area was undermined.  Beach Street was littered with overwash debris.  The parking lot at Rockport Harbor was undermined with overwash flowing over the tip of Bearskin Neck.   Thurston Place, Marmion Way, and Penzance Road were all flooded with overwash debris in the road.  The seawall was collapsed in some locations.  On Plum Island, two houses were severely undermined and damaged and collapsed into the water.  Additional houses were condemned because of damage sustained.  Along Annapolis Way in Newbury, water flowed in and around houses resulting in damaged foundations, stairs, and decks.  Dartmouth Street was inaccessible because of flooding.  Water flowed around buildings on Harvard Way.  Houses at the ends of Harvard Way and Fordham Way experienced deck and floor collapses, resulting in belongings being deposited onto the beach, water flowed around houses on these streets.  The foundation of one of the houses was undercut after a bulkhead collapsed.  In Swampscott, Lynn Shore Drive was closed due to flooding.  In Salem, Bridge and Commercial Streets were flooded with six inches of coastal water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow fell across southeastern Middlesex County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  A stationary band of even heavier snowfall persisted from southwest NH through central MA and on to the southwest across central and western CT. In those areas, reports averaged closer to 2.5 to 3 feet! Along the southeast MA coast, average amounts ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Only on Martha���s Vineyard and Nantucket were snowfall totals less than 1 foot (6 to 12 inches).  Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. The band of heaviest snowfall, with 3 to 5 inches per hour for several hours, extended from southwest NH to central and western CT.  The precipitation started as mainly snow, although a brief period of rain at the onset was common on the Islands.  Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA. Gusts exceeded hurricane force (74 mph) at a few locations.  Gale force gusts (to 50 mph) continued on the MA coast through Saturday afternoon.  The strong winds, combined with a wet snow, led to extensive power outages from downed trees and wires in southeast coastal MA and in southern RI.  Elsewhere, farther inland, the snow became drier and did not cling to trees like it did along the south and southeast coast of New England.  Some wind gusts included: 76 mph at Logan Int���l. Airport (Boston, MA), 75 mph at Bedford, MA, 77 mph at Hyannis, MA and 68 mph in Jamestown, RI.  Damaging gusts to 60 mph were recorded as far west as Worcester County, MA. However, this was an astronomically high tide given the nearness to the time of the new moon, and waves to 30 feet had built just 15 miles off the coast.  Consequently, many coastal roadways were impassable from Salisbury and Gloucester to Marshfield and Scituate on the south shore and on parts of Cape Cod.  Water several feet deep was seen flowing into some vulnerable homes in Scituate. Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Revere, Pines Road was inundated by ocean water.  Winthrop Parkway experienced splashover with ocean debris in the road.  In Winthrop, Shirley Street, Tafts Avenue, and Winthrop Shore Drive were flooded with up to two feet of water.  Yirrell Beach at Mugford Street was flooded with water flowing around and under buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Boston, Morrissey Boulevard was closed in both directions from the University of Massachusetts Boston campus to Freeport Street.  Exit 14 from Interstate 93 northbound was closed due to the flooding on Morrissey Boulevard.  In Winthrop, six roads were flooded up to the curbs with splashover reaching the front steps of houses.  Winthrop Shore Drive was flooded with water and overwash material.  In Revere, Winthrop Parkway was flooded with water and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening ocean storm spread advisory level snow across much of southern New England.  In addition...pressure falls of 12 to 15mb in 6 hours resulted in strong, damaging winds impacting Cape Cod, Nantucket, and Boston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to five inches of snow fell across western Plymouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved towards the region from the west during the evening of Friday, April 19th. Temperatures were unseasonably warm with highs in the 60s and low 70s across the region. Despite limited low level moisture in place, a line of heavy rain showers with gusty winds was located just ahead and along this approaching frontal boundary. As the line reached eastern New York and western New England, some of these showers strengthened into thunderstorms, with occasional cloud-to-cloud lightning visible. Winds on the leading edge on one of these thunderstorms caused damage to trees in the Berkshires.  The showers and thunderstorms ended in the late evening as the front moved through and to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Egremont Plain due to thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-three to twenty-eight inches of snow fell across Suffolk County.  Two people died of carbon monoxide poisoning while sitting in their cars during or after the storm. The other was a man in his 20s who was found in Mattapan in a running vehicle, the tailpipe also clogged with snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Monday, March 18th, an area of low pressure moved towards the eastern Ohio Valley.  Precipitation well out ahead of the storm's warm front moved from southwest to northeast across the region.  With enough cold air in place, the precipitation fell in the form of snow during the evening hours. By just after midnight on Tuesday, March 19th, the steady precipitation ended or changed to patchy areas of freezing drizzle or sleet.||At the same time, another storm system began to develop along the mid-Atlantic coast.  This low pressure took a track just off the Northeast coast.  The track of this storm allowed some additional moisture off the Atlantic Ocean to be drawn into the region.  During the daylight hours on Tuesday, March 19th, an additional batch of steady snowfall developed across the entire region, as an upper level shortwave moved overhead.  While some additional snow accumulated, the strength of the March sun helped limit the amount of the accumulations, especially on paved surfaces.  Some additional snow showers and squalls lingered into the evening hours, before tapering off for Tuesday night.||At the end of the storm, snowfall amounts ranged between 4 to 9 inches, with the highest amounts across the higher terrain of the Berkshires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Salisbury, coastal flooding resulted in structural damage to the ocean side of a building on North End Boulevard.  This road was flooded from #170 to #250 resulting in the mandatory evacuation of about 50 houses.  A barrier beach was breached and significant beach erosion occurred, damaging dunes and sand fencing.  The south end of Atlantic Avenue was impassable with water flowing around and under buildings.  In Gloucester, Atlantic Road was flooded and impassable.  The Good Harbor Beach walkway bridge was damaged.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging dunes.  The Lane's Cove breakwater was damaged near Dudley Street.  In Marblehead, the Marblehead Causeway (Ocean Avenue) and Marblehead Neck were flooded and impassable.  In Manchester-by-the-Sea, a pier at Magnolia Beach was damaged.  In Newbury, water flowed around and under buildings through the Beach Center parking lot and down a nearby street for about 200 yards.  Five to ten houses on Annapolis Way, Dartmouth Way, and Southern Boulevard sustained damage to the foundations and stairs.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging dunes and sand fencing.  A building on Harvard Way sustained damage to the foundation and stairs.  In Newburyport, significant beach erosion occurred.  In Rockport, numerous streets were flooded and impassable.  Water flowed around and under buildings at Front Beach and near Old Harbor.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging dunes, sand fencing, stairs and railings.  A boat ramp at Granite Pier was damaged.  In Salem, debris, rocks, and sand were strewn across the parking lot and walkways at Winter Island Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  A stationary band of even heavier snowfall persisted from southwest NH through central MA and on to the southwest across central and western CT. In those areas, reports averaged closer to 2.5 to 3 feet! Along the southeast MA coast, average amounts ranged from 1 to 2 feet.  Only on Martha���s Vineyard and Nantucket were snowfall totals less than 1 foot (6 to 12 inches).  Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. The band of heaviest snowfall, with 3 to 5 inches per hour for several hours, extended from southwest NH to central and western CT.  The precipitation started as mainly snow, although a brief period of rain at the onset was common on the Islands.  Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA. It lingered during the whole afternoon over Cape Cod and Nantucket, aided by some ocean-effect bands of snowfall.||The Blizzard of 2013 also produced a prolonged period of very strong winds Friday night along the MA and RI coasts.  Gusts exceeded hurricane force (74 mph) at a few locations.  Gale force gusts (to 50 mph) continued on the MA coast through Saturday afternoon.  The strong winds, combined with a wet snow, led to extensive power outages from downed trees and wires in southeast coastal MA and in southern RI.  Elsewhere, farther inland, the snow became drier and did not cling to trees like it did along the south and southeast coast of New England.  Some wind gusts included: 76 mph at Logan Int���l. Airport (Boston, MA), 75 mph at Bedford, MA, 77 mph at Hyannis, MA and 68 mph in Jamestown, RI.  Damaging gusts to 60 mph were recorded as far west as Worcester County, MA. Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common elsewhere in southern New England.| |In addition, moderate to major coastal flooding occurred, most notably during the time of the high tide Saturday morning along the Massachusetts east coast.  At the storm's height near the early morning low tide, the storm surge reached 3 to 4 feet along much of the MA east coast from Boston south.  At the time of the mid-morning high tide, the winds had shifted from northeast to north and the surge had dropped to 1.5 to 2.5 feet for most MA east coast locations.  However, this was an astronomically high tide given the nearness to the time of the new moon, and waves to 30 feet had built just 15 miles off the coast.  Consequently, many coastal roadways were impassable from Salisbury and Gloucester to Marshfield and Scituate on the south shore and on parts of Cape Cod.  Water several feet deep was seen flowing into some vulnerable homes in Scituate.  Although there was some structural damage, it did not come close to what was experienced during the Blizzard of 1978.  Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easy and Washington Streets were flooded up to three feet deep.  Water up to two feet deep came up to a couple of houses on Beach Street.  Wauwinet Road near Eat Fire Springs Road flooded.  Water was as far up as the Nantucket Hotel on Easton Street in the Brant Point area.  Main Street was flooded up to the Club Car Restaurant.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging sand fencing.

Significant beach erosion occurred with damage to dunes, sand fencing, and stairs.  The Pilgrim Sands Motel was flooded with two feet of water in their basement.  The Breakwater parking lot was undermined and eroded.  In Scituate, numerous streets were flooded and impassable, with up to four feet of water.  Moderate to major coastal flooding occurred with depths of water reaching up to 16.6 feet at one point.  Water had flowed up to an apartment building on Cole Parkway.  The Scituate light house was damaged.  Water was flowing around and under houses near Edward Foster Road, Town Way Extension, and Oceanside Road.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging dunes, sand fencing, and stairs.  A house on Scituate Avenue was flooded.  The foundation of two buildings were damaged, one at the intersection of Oceanside Road and Tuner Road and the other on Surfside Avenue.  A large hole in Glades Road exposed buried utility lines.  Ocean debris was found on Surfside Road. In Marshfield, numerous streets were flooded and impassable.  Cars were stuck in flood waters on Plymouth Avenue.  Water flowed around and under buildings throughout Marshfield.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging stairs.  Brook Street was washed out.  In Duxbury, significant beach erosion occurred, damaging stairs and dunes.  Damage was sustained to the stairs of a building on Ocean Road North.  Water flowed around and under buildings in the High Pines area.  Water flowed through one house on Gurnet Road.  In addition, stairs were damaged on about 15 buildings on Gurnet Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Monday, March 18th, an area of low pressure moved towards the eastern Ohio Valley.  Precipitation well out ahead of the storm's warm front moved from southwest to northeast across the region.  With enough cold air in place, the precipitation fell in the form of snow during the evening hours. By just after midnight on Tuesday, March 19th, the steady precipitation ended or changed to patchy areas of freezing drizzle or sleet.||At the same time, another storm system began to develop along the mid-Atlantic coast.  This low pressure took a track just off the Northeast coast.  The track of this storm allowed some additional moisture off the Atlantic Ocean to be drawn into the region.  During the daylight hours on Tuesday, March 19th, an additional batch of steady snowfall developed across the entire region, as an upper level shortwave moved overhead.  While some additional snow accumulated, the strength of the March sun helped limit the amount of the accumulations, especially on paved surfaces.  Some additional snow showers and squalls lingered into the evening hours, before tapering off for Tuesday night.||At the end of the storm, snowfall amounts ranged between 4 to 9 inches, with the highest amounts across the higher terrain of the Berkshires.

Route 6A was flooded and impassable at the Dennis/Brewster line.  A parking lot at Corporation Beach was undermined and had collapsed.  Beach erosion occurred, damaging sand fencing.  Dr Botero Road was flooded and had washed out in places.  In Borne, significant beach erosion occurred, damaging stairs of four buildings.  The town access ramp at Phillips Road was washed away.  In Brewster, approximately 20 feet of the parking lot at Ellis landing was lost as the beach washed over the parking lot.  A house adjacent to the parking lot sustained damage to the structure and foundation.  Fifteen feet of parking lot at Breakwater Beach was lost.  Significant beach erosion occurred, damaging dunes, sand fencing, and stairs.  Approximately 10 feet of parking lot at Mants Landing was lost.  In Chatham, significant beach erosion occurred, damaging sand fencing and stairs.  Water flowed under and around buildings at the Fish Pier.  Debris accumulated at the northern end of the pier.  A seasonal cottage on North Beach Island lost its foundation support and stairs and was leaning on its side.  It has been condemned and scheduled for demolition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening ocean storm spread advisory level snow across much of southern New England.  In addition...pressure falls of 12 to 15mb in 6 hours resulted in strong, damaging winds impacting Cape Cod, Nantucket, and Boston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Nantucket Memorial Airport (KACK) recorded sustained winds up to 43 mph and gusts to 58 mph.  No damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observation System at Provincetown Municipal Airport (KPVC) recorded sustained winds of 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved off the mid-Atlantic coast during the early morning of Thursday, March 7th. Although this storm system was far south of the region, it moved very slowly and help produced a strong easterly flow, which transported moisture off the Atlantic Ocean into the region.||As an upper level shortwave moved from the Great Lakes region towards the Northeast coast, it interacted with the moisture, allowing snowfall to develop over the area. Initially, the snowfall was rather spotty in nature and temperatures just above freezing prevented much accumulation from occurring during the morning and afternoon hours of March 7th. However, a steadier, accumulating snow developed across the entire region by the evening hours as the shortwave moved just south of the region across Pennsylvania and New Jersey and temperatures began to cool.||Steady snowfall, moderate in intensity at times, occurred during the overnight hours and into the morning of Friday, March 8th, as the upper level shortwave became absorbed by the much larger storm off the coast. Snowfall tapered off to snow showers around midday on Friday, March 8th. By that point, storm total snowfall range from around 5 inches in the lower elevations to 11 inches in the higher terrain of the Berkshires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day on May 29th, a warm front lifted northward across western Massachusetts. Although the day began cloudy, some breaks of sun started to occur by early afternoon. At the same time, a potent upper level disturbance over Ontario started to approach the region and a strong jet streak was located just to the north. With a moist southerly flow moving into the region at the surface, it became rather humid as well.||With all these ingredients coming together, thunderstorms developed across upstate New York by mid afternoon and began to spread eastward. The thunderstorms reached Berkshire County by the evening hours. Many of the thunderstorms organized into a strong squall line, producing wind damage. These thunderstorms produced a significant amount of lightning, as well, which in combination with the wind damage, led to some power outages across the area.||With the region already rather saturated from repeated thunderstorms over the past week, the additional heavy rainfall that occurred caused flash flooding in some areas. This caused roads to be closed due to being impassable by flowing water.  Portions of Williamstown were hit quite hard, as at least eight roads were damaged due to flooding.  Two homes were without road access as a result of the flooding.||Thunderstorms pushed east of the area by around midnight, ending the threat of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in North Adams as a result of heavy rainfall from thunderstorms.  State Street was impassable due to flooding and was temporarily closed.
