EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sandwich experienced the majority of the coastal flooding on Cape Cod.  The Sandwich Boardwalk was flooded.  At Sandwich Marina, water was up to the harbormaster shack.  Foster Road, Spring Hill Road, and River Street flooded.  Houses on Salt Marsh Road were damaged, including a deck destroyed and a foundation undermined.  In addition, several houses were flooded.  An elevated walkway connecting the town beach parking area and the barrier beaches was destroyed.  Minor coastal flooding occurred in Eastham, Wellfleet, Brewster, and Chatham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Plymouth, Route 3A was closed between Cliff Street and Clifford Road due to flooding.  A septic system was exposed on the seaward side of Taylor Avenue because of erosion.  Warren Avenue near the Bert's Landing Restaurant was flooded.  Taylor Avenue near Bartlett Pond was flooded.  A sidewalk was undermined at Nelson Street Beach.  Homer Avenue was flooded with so much sand it was almost impassable by car.  In the Long Beach area, rocks and other ocean debris covered almost a quarter of a mile of Route 3A.  Parking lots in this area were at least 50% flooded with lots of rocks and ocean debris and jersey barriers from the edges of the lot were pushed in towards the center of the lot.  In Scituate, the parking lot at Scituate Light was flooded.  Rocks and other debris were reported near a fire hydrant in the parking lot.  Houses were damaged in this area as well.  The Town Way extension was impassable.  Part of Edward Foster Road was closed.  The upper parking area at the Cole Parkway Marina was flooded with between 1 and 2 feet of water.  Central Avenue was flooded and impassable with debris and rocks on the road.  Buildings were damaged along Central Avenue, especially near that roads intersection with Cliff Road.    Turner Road was flooded with two feet of water near Oceanside Drive.  Edward Foster Road between 1st and 2nd cliff flooded.  Oceanside Drive was flooded and impassable.  Cars were stuck in coastal flood waters on Atlantic Avenue.  Cole Parkway and Atlantic Drive were flooded and impassable.  Barratt Street was washed out.  Peggoty Beach experienced significant erosion.  Patios, decks, and other sorts of exterior structure were damaged in the area of Surfside Road.  In Marshfield, the Brant Rock and Esplanade sections were flooded and impassable with rocks and other debris on roadways.  Ocean Street from Colonial Road to Dyke Road had rocks and other debris in the road from significant splashover.  Damage to the seawall at Foster Avenue and Brook Street resulted in those streets flooding.  Numerous coastal streets were flooded with water flowing around the buildings and rocks and other ocean debris on the roads.  In Hull, Atlantic Avenue at Gunrock Avenue was closed for six hours because of at least six inches of water flowing over the road.  Ocean Avenue at Spring Street was flooded and impassable.  Significant erosion of dunes occurred at Nantasket Beach.  Houses and roads along Gunrock Beach were damaged.  A playground on Ocean Avenue was flooded.  Nantasket Beach between W and Y Streets was flooded, with ocean water and sand flowing through houses.  Nantasket Avenue at several locations  was flooded with water and ocean debris.  Warren Street at Nantasket Beach experienced flooding and erosion, exposing concrete foundations.  A sidewalk was undermined and destroyed on Franklin Street near Nantasket Beach.  A side street and a driveway along that side street were washed out near Gunrock Avenue.  This resulted in damage to nearby buildings.  Yards were flooded on both sides of Hampton Circle.  Hull Shore Drive was closed due to flooding.  George Washington Avenue was flooded with water and ocean debris.  A house in the Rockland Street marsh was flooded with water around the house and in the basement.  In Duxbury, Gurnet Road was washed out stranding people in the Gurnet section of Plymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid air mass was in place across western Massachusetts on Tuesday, May 21st. A slow moving frontal boundary was situated just north of the area across the Saint Lawrence River Valley along the United States-Canadian border. During the day, a wave of low pressure moved along this boundary, allowing the boundary to slowly push southward towards the area. With strong wind shear aloft and plenty of low level moisture in place, thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across eastern New York and some of these moved into western Massachusetts by early evening.||Thunderstorms initially formed as single cells and supercells, but eventually organized into several broken lines, which moved from west to east across the area. A few of the thunderstorms that moved across western Massachusetts produced wind damage to trees and power lines, as well as quarter-size hail.||As the surface wave of low pressure moved away and with the loss of daytime heating, thunderstorms eventually ended during the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Police reported that 10 to 15 trees were sheared off and wires were knocked down due to thunderstorm winds near Alford.  The downed trees and wires caused Route 71 to be closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail two miles north of Alander.  The hail fell to a depth of five inches and destroyed several tents at a YMCA Camp Ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on Sunday, June 2nd with temperatures well into the 80s. A cold front and associated pre-frontal surface trough was moving towards the area during the afternoon hours. With plenty of moisture and instability in place, showers and thunderstorms developed across the region. These thunderstorms were aided by very strong winds aloft and one storm became severe across western Massachusetts, producing wind damage to utility wires. The thunderstorms weakened by evening as the cold front moved across the area, ending the threat for severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were reported down as a result of thunderstorm winds in Ashley Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light to moderate snowfall occurred during the daytime hours on Tuesday, December 17th. Snowfall began during the morning hours as a weak upper level disturbance passed south of the region. This upper level disturbance caused a surface low to develop off the coast of New Jersey during the afternoon hours, which helped produce some additional snowfall into the afternoon hours. This low headed eastward out to sea through the afternoon. By the time snow ended in the late afternoon or early evening hours, about 2 to 6 inches of snow fell across much of the region. ||Temperatures during the snowfall were very cold, with many areas only in the single digits. This allowed for very slick driving conditions during the busy holiday shopping season. Numerous traffic accidents were reported as a result of the snowfall.

This low headed eastward out to sea through the afternoon. On the morning hours of Thursday, Februrary 8th, an area of low pressure rapidly developed off the mid-Atlantic coast. The development of this storm was aided by the phasing with another strong upper level disturbance moving towards the coast from the Great Lakes region. As this developing storm moved northeast off the Northeast coast from the late morning of February 8th and into the overnight hours, a large amount of Atlantic moisture was pulled westward, producing a widespread heavy snowfall. ||The snow fell heaviest during the evening and into the overnight hours, with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour at times. In addition, strong northeast winds allowed for blowing and drifting of snowfall. During the height of the storm, winds gusted up to 36 mph across Berkshire County.||The snowfall tapered off during the morning hours, although blowing and drifting continued through the day on Saturday, February 9th.  Total snowfall amounts ranged from 10 inches in northern parts of Berkshire County to nearly two feet in southeastern parts of Berkshire county.||Travel was significantly affected across the state of Massachusetts.  In addition, nearly 400,000 customers lost power across the entire state.

An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Taunton Municipal Airport (KTAN) recorded a wind gust of 47 mph.  Elsewhere in northern Bristol County, damage indicated wind gusts were likely higher at times.  A tree was downed onto wires on Bay State Road in Rehoboth.  In Mansfield, wires were downed onto a school bus at the intersection of North and East Streets.  Several trees and wires were downed in Norton, resulting in power outages.  In Taunton, a tree and wires were downed onto a  house on Prospect Place.

Low pressure moved across Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.  The highest snow amounts were across northeastern Massachusetts where a heavy band of snow set up right around the evening rush hour.  This band of snow produced snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to eight inches of snow fell across northern Worcester County, with the lighter amounts across the western parts of the county.

This band of snow produced snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across northwestern Middlesex County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, MA (KMQE) recorded sustained wind speeds of 44 mph and gusts up to 63 mph.  In addition, large limbs were downed onto Route 37 in Braintree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated Surface Observing Systems at Chatham (CQX) and Hyannis (HYA) recorded wind gusts of 50 and 48 mph, respectively.  In addition, amateur radio operators recorded wind gusts of 61 and 56 mph on their home weather stations in Marston's Mills and Barnstable, respectively.  A telephone pole was downed across Mill Pond Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England, resulting in strong cold air advection and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines on South Franklin Street in Holbrook were downed by strong winds.  In Randolph, a large tree limb was downed onto utility lines along Park Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Ohio Valley towards western New York on Saturday, December 14th. At the same time, another area of low pressure began to develop over the mid-Atlantic coast late in the day and rapidly strengthened overnight as it moved up the eastern seaboard. This storm was located off the coast of New England during the day on Sunday, December 15th. ||As a result, snow slowly spread from southwest to northeast during the day on December 14th. With a cold and dry airmass in place, the snow was initially light in intensity and little accumulation occurred during the day. ||However, as the secondary low began to rapidly develop, a steadier and heavier snowfall moved across the region during the evening hours and into the overnight. Snow fell at rates in excess of one inch per hour over much of the region.  During the overnight hours, some sleet mixed in with the snow, especially across southern parts of Berkshire County. ||The bulk of the accumulating snow was finished by the late overnight hours, but light snow showers and flurries continued into the mid-morning hours. By the end of the storm, snowfall amounts ranged from nearly around 6 inches in Pittsfield to about 11 inches in Adams.

A warm front moved northward across southern New England Sunday night (Oct 6) into Monday (Oct 7) creating a warm, moist, unstable atmosphere over the region.  Monday afternoon and evening (Oct 7) a strong cold front brought a line of strong to severe showers and storms through southern New England.  Strong, damaging winds occurred both with the storms and in the tight pressure gradient ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires on Holyoke Street were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Logan International Airport (KBOS) recorded sustained wind speeds of 33 mph.  A tree was downed onto a car at the intersection of Albany and Webber Streets in Boston.  A tree was downed onto Commonwealth Avenue, blocking one lane of the road.  A tree and wires were downed onto Washington Avenue in Chelsea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved over southern New England bringing showers and thunderstorms to the region.  Precipitable water values over two inches indicated a very moist atmosphere; in addition, weak winds at the mid levels resulted in very slow moving storms.  Both of these things led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.  Convergence at the surface and a significantly unstable environment resulted in a shower producing a waterspout over the Quabbin Reservoir in New Salem, Massachusetts.  A Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation (DCR) employee at the reservoir was able to snap several photos of the waterspout and reported that it never moved onto land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9 was flooded in both directions at Route 126, making Route 9 impassable.  Speen Street at Cochituate Road was also flooded with a car stuck in the flood waters there.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms developed over the Wellesley/Needham area.  These storms moved very slowly, if at all, producing three to four inches of rain over the region in 1 to 2 hours time.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding across both cities.  Several streets, including a few major highways were closed due to flooding including:  Washington Street, Route 9, Route 16, Route 128 at exit 19, Great Plain Avenue, and Weston Road.  Many cars were stuck in flood waters on these roads and across intersections.  Numerous basements also were flooded in this region.

Southern New England was sandwiched in between this and high pressure off the coast to the southeast, creating a very tight pressure gradient over the region.  This resulted in strong gusty winds which caused some damage, especially to areas where the trees were still fully leaved.  A few locations sustained higher wind gusts and thus more damage.  These were mainly along the east coast of Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Logan International Airport in East Boston (KBOS) recorded sustained wind speeds of 39 mph and gusts to 51 mph.  Wires were downed onto Ashford and Linden Streets in Boston.  A large branch was downed onto a car in the Back Bay, damaging the car.  A tree was downed onto wires on Readville Street in Hyde Park.  A large section of a roof was peeled off by the wind in Chelsea.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation employee observed a waterspout on Quabbin Reservoir in New Salem, MA.  He was able to snap two pictures of the storm, one showing a funnel and another showing the funnel extended down to the water.  The time has been approximated based on radar imagery.  The waterspout was very short lived, never hit land, and did no damage and injured no people.  Winds aloft were not conducive for tornadic development, but the environment was unstable and a surface front was moving through the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved through southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of the area. Some of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds. Also precipitable water values were near two inches, allowing for heavy rain with all of these storms, which resulted in a few instances of poor drainage flooding.
A backdoor cold front pushing south through New England produced isolated severe thunderstorms in western and central Massachusetts during the afternoon and evening.

A couple of upper level disturbances moved over southern New England while at the surface a slow moving warm front drifted north across the area.  This ignited showers and thunderstorms across much of the area.  Southwest flow at the surface provided a deep layer of moisture with high dewpoints and high precipitable water values, which resulted in heavy rainfall with the showers and thunderstorms.  Anywhere from one to four inches of rain fell with these storms, sometimes within a very short period of time, which resulted in flash flooding in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The westbound lane of Route 9 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved into southern New England, providing lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop in a hot and humid atmosphere.  In addition, CAPE up to 2000 J/kg and 30-40 kts of shear provided enough instability and shear for thunderstorms to become severe.  Because of warm air aloft, these thunderstorms mostly produced strong to damaging winds and not large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a detached garage on Bonnie Avenue, igniting a fire.  There were no vehicles in the garage but there was damage to other household possessions and belongings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormal heat for early September coupled with an elevated mixed layer resulted in a few strong to severe thunderstorms developing late in the day in western Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire.  These storms produced damaging winds, downing several trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large limb on West Street was downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Wood Street and Ashley Boulevard was flooded with at least six inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and cooling air in the upper levels resulted in steepening mid-level lapse rates, producing showers and thunderstorms.  A warm, unstable environment allowed these storms to become severe, with several storms producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell along Interstate 291.
